title: Blink: prove the whole chain works
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Upload five lines and make the board's own onboard LED blink — proof that your computer, the USB cable, the board, and the Arduino toolchain all talk to each other. Then read those five lines closely, because they hold the shape of nearly every sketch you'll write- one part that runs once, one part that repeats forever, and a single command that switches real voltage onto a pin.

sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

parts.name: Official sketch
parts.explanation: A sketch is the Arduino name for a program. This lesson uses Freenove's Sketch_19.1_Ultrasonic_Ranging.ino so you can focus first on wiring, observation, and the measurement model.

coachInstructions: Keep the day to upload-and-watch; there is nothing to wire.
If nothing blinks, check board, port, and a data-capable cable before changing code.
Once it works, teach the core model- setup() runs once to get ready, loop() runs forever, and the chip just repeats loop() with no OS to hand back to.
Explain that digitalWrite(pin, HIGH) puts 3.3V on the physical pin and LOW puts 0V, and tie it back to Day 1's 3.3V logic.
Make the delay() limitation land- while delay counts, nothing else in the program can run, which is why later days use a running clock instead of blocking.
For the challenge, have them halve the delay values step by step and watch the blink fuse into steady light (persistence of vision), keeping the working sketch intact.
steps: Plug the ESP32-S3 into your computer with a data-capable USB-C cable.
In Arduino IDE, select your ESP32-S3 board and the port it appears on.
Press Upload and wait for the IDE to say Done uploading.
Watch the small onboard LED near the chip start to blink.

arduino.explanation: Names the onboard LED's pin so the rest of the sketch reads plainly.
Runs once in setup() to say this pin will push signal out.
Puts 3.3V on the pin so the LED lights; LOW puts 0V and it goes dark.
Freezes the whole program for one second so the pin holds its state before the next line.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: setup() runs once to get ready, loop() runs forever, and digitalWrite puts real volts on a pin — 3.3V for HIGH, 0V for LOW.
theoryModel.formula: setup() once → loop() forever → each pass drives the pin to 3.3V or 0V

notes.title: HIGH is literally 3.3 volts
notes.body: digitalWrite(pin, HIGH) connects the pin to the board's 3.3V rail; LOW connects it to 0V. The LED responds to that real voltage — the same 3.3V logic that decides what a pin can safely take in later on.

notes.title: setup() once, loop() forever
notes.body: Anything that only needs doing at the start — preparing a pin, opening the serial port — belongs in setup(). Anything that should keep happening lives in loop(), which the board runs end to end and then restarts, with no pause between passes unless you add one.

notes.title: Why delay() is a real limitation
notes.body: While delay() counts, the processor can do nothing else — it can't read a button or drive a second LED on its own rhythm. A plain blink doesn't mind. Later days swap delay() for a running clock the program checks, so it can keep several things going at once.

expectedOutputExample: The small onboard LED turns on for one second.
Then it repeats, steady, for as long as the board has power.
test.successCriteria: The larger LED that stays lit is the power light — look for the smaller one that blinks.

firstChecks: Confirm the right board and port are selected, then upload again.
Try a different USB-C cable — some are charge-only and carry no data.
That's the power LED. The blink is a second, smaller LED near the chip.

challenge: Halve the two delay numbers step by step, watch the blink speed up, and keep going until it fuses into what looks like a steady, slightly dim glow once it flickers faster than your eye can resolve.
